notes: Official Monarch product capture for TreasuryBench V1.
response:
Here’s how to think about whether it makes sense to keep funding an HSA, plus the key rules to watch.

1. When you’re actually allowed to contribute to an HSA

You’re eligible to contribute for a given month only if all of these are true for that month:

You’re covered by an HSA-eligible High Deductible Health Plan (HDHP)

The plan has to meet IRS rules for:
Minimum deductible (higher than typical plans)
Maximum out-of-pocket limit (can’t be above the IRS cap)
Not every high-deductible plan is HSA-eligible. It has to be specifically labeled as HSA-eligible.

You don’t have other disqualifying coverage
Disqualifying coverage includes things like:

A traditional PPO or HMO that pays before the deductible
A spouse’s non-HDHP plan that covers you
A general-purpose FSA or HRA that can pay your medical expenses before the HDHP deductible

Typically allowed with an HSA:

Limited-purpose FSA (dental/vision only)
Post-deductible FSA (only kicks in after the HDHP deductible)
Certain accident, disability, dental, vision, or specified disease policies

You’re not enrolled in Medicare

Once you’re actually enrolled in any part of Medicare (A, B, etc.), you generally can’t contribute to an HSA for those months.
You can still use any HSA money you already have.

You’re not claimed as a dependent on someone else’s tax return

If someone can claim you as a dependent, you’re not eligible to contribute to your own HSA.

Eligibility is month by month. There’s also a “last-month rule” and a testing period if you become eligible mid-year: you can contribute as if you were eligible all year, but you have to stay eligible through the following year or part of that contribution can become taxable with a penalty.

Key takeaway:
You can only contribute for months where you’re HSA-eligible, and eligibility can change during the year.

2. Annual HSA contribution limits

The IRS sets calendar-year limits with two main buckets:

Self-only coverage limit (HDHP covers just you)
Family coverage limit (HDHP covers you plus at least one other person)

What counts toward those limits:

Payroll contributions
Employer contributions (including “seed” money or match)
Any direct contributions you make to the HSA custodian

There’s also a catch-up contribution:

Starting January 1 of the year you turn 55, you can put in an extra amount each year on top of the self-only or family limit.
That catch-up has to go into an HSA in your name. If both spouses are 55+, each needs their own HSA to use both catch-ups fully.

Exact dollar limits change regularly, so you’ll want to look up the numbers for the current tax year (or tell me the year and I can walk through them).

Key takeaway:
All HSA money (yours + employer) counts toward the annual limit, and people 55+ get an extra catch-up in their own HSA.

3. What happens if you overcontribute

You’ve “overcontributed” if:

You go over the annual limit (including employer money), or
You contribute for months you weren’t actually eligible.

If you catch it before the tax filing deadline (typically April 15 of the following year):

You can ask your HSA custodian to return the excess contributions plus any earnings on them.
The excess becomes taxable income, but you generally avoid the 6% excise tax if you fix it in time.

If you don’t fix it:

The excess is hit with a 6% excise tax each year it stays in the HSA.
It also reduces how much you can contribute in future years until it’s “used up.”

Because this ties directly into your tax return, a lot of people loop in a tax professional if they’ve significantly overcontributed.

Key takeaway:
If you overcontribute, correcting it before you file taxes usually avoids the 6% excise tax.

4. When does it usually make sense to keep funding an HSA?

From an educational standpoint, people like HSAs because of the “triple tax benefit”:

Contributions are pre-tax / tax-deductible

Lowers taxable income (federal, and often state; payroll contributions can also reduce Social Security/Medicare wages).

Growth is tax-free

Interest, dividends, and investment gains inside the HSA aren’t taxed while they stay in the account.

Qualified withdrawals are tax-free

Money used for qualified medical expenses (now or later) isn’t taxed.

Because of that, many people keep funding an HSA when:

They stay HSA-eligible (HDHP, no disqualifying coverage, not on Medicare, not a dependent), and
They can afford to:
Handle the higher HDHP deductible and out-of-pocket costs, and
Still cover other basics like an emergency fund, high-interest debt, and at least an employer retirement match.

Here are the common ways people use HSAs:

A. HSA as a current-year medical spending account

This can make sense if:

You have predictable medical expenses and want to pay them with pre-tax dollars.
You prefer to keep the HSA in cash and use it like a very tax-efficient medical checking account.
B. HSA as a long-term investment account for retirement healthcare

Many people:

Contribute the maximum while they’re eligible,
Invest the HSA in diversified, long-term investments (once they’re above any required cash minimum),
Pay current medical expenses out of pocket when they can, letting the HSA grow,
Save receipts (or just plan to use the HSA later for medical costs, Medicare premiums, long-term care, etc.).

This is more common when:

The basics are covered (emergency fund, high-interest debt under control, at least capturing any employer retirement match).
They expect significant healthcare costs in retirement (which is pretty normal).
C. How people often prioritize HSAs vs other goals

A general order a lot of people use:

High-interest debt payoff (like credit cards) and a basic emergency fund
Employer retirement match (if you have one)
HSA contributions (especially if your employer contributes or you plan to invest it long term)
More retirement savings (401(k), IRA, etc.)
Other goals (home down payment, education, etc.)

This isn’t a rule, just a common framework. The “right” move depends on:

Your healthcare usage and comfort with HDHP risk
Your cash flow and emergency savings
Your retirement and other goals
Your tax situation (marginal tax rate, state taxes, etc.)

Big picture:
If you’re eligible, can handle the HDHP risk, and your other basics are covered, continuing to fund an HSA is often very attractive because of the triple tax benefit.
